Champion
Ally
Enlist
Rally

Endorse
Reciprocity
What is in it for your team is a cleaner forecast.
What would have to be true for this to work for you?
Could I get you to champion this early in the room?
Let us tie this to the target we all share.
I will support your rollout first, so you can count on me too.
Victor, I will be honest, my team is buried. Whatever this rollout is, we do not have the capacity for it.
Fair, and I am not here to add work. Honestly, what is in it for your team is fewer last-minute fire drills, the rollout fixes the handoffs that keep burning your people.
That part I would actually like. But I cannot have my schedule blown up by someone else plan.
Then you shape it, not me. What would have to be true for this to work for your team, so that it fits your schedule instead of fighting it?
If the rollout phased in over two months instead of all at once, I could absorb it. Okay, that I can work with.
Done, we phase it. And here is my real ask: could I get you to champion the phased version in Thursday meeting? Your word carries more weight in that room than mine.
I can do that. If it is phased and it is framed as ours, I will back it, and I will bring finance along.
Then let us tie it to the regional target we all own, it stops being my project and becomes ours. And I will back your warehouse upgrade next quarter, so this runs both ways.
Now that is a deal I will actually advocate for. Phased, shared, and reciprocal, count me in.
I enlisted one ally before the meeting and got her to champion the idea, which swayed the room.
I rallied the teams around a shared goal, and through reciprocity they endorsed it without being told.
What is in it for your team is fewer last-minute fire drills.
I will support your project first, so you can count on me too.

Okay, think this through before the cross-functional meeting. I need three teams on board for the regional rollout, and not one of them reports to me. I cannot order anyone. So if I walk in and just announce the plan, I will get polite nods and zero follow-through. Here is the plan to actually win them over. First, I find the win for each of them before I ask for anything. For finance, the angle is a cleaner forecast; for IT, fewer last-minute fire drills; for operations, a workload they can plan around. People back what helps them, so I lead with their benefit, not mine. Second, I do not give orders, I frame. Instead of saying do this, I get them to see it as their own idea. I ask what would have to be true for this to work for your team, so that they shape the how and own the outcome. Third, I never go in cold. I enlist one ally first, usually the person with the most credibility in the room, and I get them to champion the idea early. One advocate in the room is worth ten slides. Fourth, I tie everything to a goal we already share, so that it stops being my project and becomes our project. And I remember reciprocity: I show up for their priorities first, and support comes back later. Find the win, frame it, enlist an ally, rally around the shared goal. That is how you move the org when you cannot pull rank.

Hi Victor, it's Renata. Quick thought before your cross-functional push, because influencing without authority is the skill that separates a manager from a leader, and most people get it backwards. Three things. First, stop trying to be convincing and start being useful. Before you ask anyone for anything, work out what they actually want and lead with that. People do not act because your logic is strong; they act because they see what is in it for them. Find the win for the other side first, every time. Second, do not push, pull. You cannot order a peer, so do not try. Get them to arrive at the conclusion themselves by asking the right questions, so that the plan feels like theirs, not yours. A plan someone owns gets done; a plan someone was told to follow quietly dies. Third, and this is where strong managers slip, never walk into the room cold. Enlist one credible ally beforehand and have them champion the idea, so the momentum is already there when you arrive. And bank goodwill early through reciprocity. Support other people's priorities before you need theirs, and they will rally to yours when it counts. Be useful, pull instead of push, line up an advocate, and tie it to a goal you both share. Do that and you will move the whole org without a single line on the org chart.
